comments.title: What to do after I forget to invoke Mercury
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Based on your public advice to somebody else, I have been trying to invoke Mercury via chants and charity donations in multiples of 8 each Wednesday. I completed three or four groups of eight week cycles (so 3-4 months of this), and was on week 5 of the most recent cycle but ended up forgetting to do anything last Wednesday.

So my question is: What do I do now? Do I just recontinue this Wednesday as if nothing happened and then add another week at the end of this cycle? Do I just consider the last four weeks to be a waste and restart this Wednesday from scratch? Do I just double the amount I donate this week and continue onto Week 6? Do I need to make any other offerings/apologies/etc?

Any insights would help.

As an aside, I don't know if I was getting much by way of obvious blessings by invoking Mercury to begin with, but since missing last Wednesday, a whole string of unlikely, strange, and annoying (but not devastating) events related to travel and technology have been occurring to me. Because I am starting other work as well, maybe it is unrelated to Mercury in particular, but it sure has been weird!

replies.title: Re: What to do after I forget to invoke Mercury
replies.content: Do you do any form of divination? If so, the first question to ask is whether invoking Mercury is a good idea for you just now. The second is what to do in the current situation. If the divination is favorable, I'd expect to make a double donation this week and then go on -- but you'll want to check. (You can get someone else to do a divination for you if you like.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I had a very weird experience a couple nights ago. Someone I used to be quite close with came to me in a dream. She told me she had just died (and sure enough, when I went looking for it, I found her obituary), and she came to say goodbye. Here’s where things get really strange though: she said we would probably never see each other again. Considering we destroyed our old friend group because we could not admit we were in love, I found this odd because I was sure we’d have karma, and told her as much.

She told me we probably did, but that she was finally going home. I will remain here, as a human, and she is going to go back home, wherever that is: and she said that it will be almost impossible, if not actually impossible for us to cross paths again for a long time, if we ever do. She gave me a hug, said she truly wished me the best, and then we were suddenly at a train station, and she said goodbye as she boarded the train

She always seemed a little odd, almost as if she wasn’t quite comfortable with the basics of being a human being; but I’d long thought that was because of her extremely abusive upbringing. I find this dream quite unsettling, because it raises all kinds of strange and troubling questions, such as where did she come from? We hit it off as soon as we met, in a way that suggests to me that we had some long standing karma, so how long was she here?

How seriously should I take this dream?

If I should take it seriously, I'm troubled even more than just the one person because it looked like that train was very crowded, and there were quite a few more people on the platform who seemed to be getting ready to board it....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I mean it very broadly indeed. The concept that the world we experience is characterized by suffering and evil, and we should limit our involvement in life in order to prepare ourselves to go to a heavenly world where we actually belong, is (according to this visionary experience) a reflection of the experience of these souls, trapped in a world foreign to them. Not only Christianity but the entire panoply of religions that place humanity's proper destiny beyond the world -- post-Exilic Judaism, Islam, most currents of Buddhism, and the list goes on -- are shaped by the experience of these souls.

What's more, for them, it's a wholly accurate statement (again, assuming for a moment the accuracy of the vision). They did indeed fall into material embodiment due to a mistaken choice; they are indeed caught in a world that will always be difficult, painful, and inimical to them, and burdened with material bodies that will always feel unnatural to them; and their best option is to return to their rightful home. It's just that none of this is true for the majority of human souls, who are native to this planet, and who naturally seek not transcendence, but the healing and blessing that comes from the immanent presence of the Divine.

As for who belongs in that category, I'm hardly in a position to pass judgment on anyone's soul! The one thing I know is that I've never been able to fit my mind or my heart around the notion that I should despise the earth and my own body, and long for a luminous otherworld where I supposedly belong. I belong here.

replies.title: Re: Vision of population collapse
replies.content:
Fascinating scry JMG! I didn't realize you'd had this vision. Oddly it does square with certain things I've heard from various yogis over the years including Sadhguru.

I did learn the following things from these yogis:

1. A huge number of souls are about to exit this planet forever. Sadhguru himself said part of what he has planned for the 3 billion or so people who have current and past life connections to his yogic lineage are going to leave this planet for good. He literally said part of his commission was, "to clean up the planet.". All of the people who've participated in Inner Engineering, visited Dyana Linga temple or any of the satellite temples he's built on other continents will be among those who will be able to permenantly exit. If they don't leave that means they're part of the remnent who've volunteered to stay behind to help future humans evolve themselves spiritually. The animals will someday spiritually evolve enough to incarnate as humans and the remnant will help by being spiritual mentors and guides.

2. I keep hearing various tantriks and Vedic Astrologers all insisting that the next grand spiritual Islamic leader has already been born in the Middle East. Some of these people I listen to put this future spiritual leader on the spiritual evolution level of Shiva, Krishna or Buddha - he's that advanced spiritually himself. In other words - (supposedly) an Avatar of the Divine Planes now resides quietly somewhere in the Middle East.

They say he's quite young right now - like pre-teen or teen from the way they sound - but once he turns 40 the Vedic stars say he'll light up the Ummah worldwide and especially the Middle East in a bright, glorious blaze and Islam will be completely and permanently changed forever. So much so that once he's done the Islam of the 22nd century worldwide and beyond will look absolutely nothing like the Islam of today.

3. Rajarshi Nandy is just one Yogi I watch who says alien beings are constantly incarnating as humans. Not only that but plenty of them actually compete to incarnate here. The reason is that spiritual evolution is super-fast on Earth compared to much more blissful, happy places. Yes, a lot of those incarnations suck but there is a very quiet upside for enduring the suckage. New possibilities and new pathways for divine evolution open up for those higher beings (and presumably for native humans too) that are not available on their own planes. So yeah...it's a hardship incarnation but it's not as if there's zero reward for enduring the ugly parts.

4. According to Sadhguru JMG's scry vision is correct. Our solar system has "turned a corner" so to speak and is coming closer to another solar system again. The next 1200 years is a stair step out of kali yuga into dwapara yuga.

If one goes by Krishna's definition of a yuga in the Bhagavad Gita instead of the author of the Purana then each yuga is an equal astronomical unit of 12,000 years rather than the Puranas definition of 4-3-2-1. So the climb will take 1200 transition years. I think the Purana author might have been talking about a grander cycle than the one Krishna was talking about. I think Krishna was just talking about our Milky Way galaxy and how long each 'season' lasts with some stair steps between each yuga.

Once our solar system draws close to this other solar system humans will seemingly have new capabilities even in their physical bodies that can only be dreamed of or hinted about right now. One example Sadhguru said will happen is that humans will start seeing completely new colors in the color spectrum. New, beautiful colors will seemingly be everywhere and people will wonder about how they suddenly showed up. Ditto for other "newly evolved" capabilities. Although they're not really newly evolved. Rather humanity has always had these capabilities but in Kali yuga they are dormant. They need the proximity to the other solar system to start up again. Sadhguru says these seemingly newly evolved capabilities will lead to new discoveries and inventions that are not possible in our time.

Sadhguru said the difference will be like seeing stark differences in each of the four seasons in temperate zones. Look at our planet in a temperate latitude covered in snow in winter. If you looked at the same area in springtime in bloom and didn't know any better you would not believe you were looking at the same region. The difference the other solar system's proximity makes will have that big of a difference on human capabilities in 1200 years time at the dawn of Dwapara yuga.

Now...what humanity does with those seemingly new capabilities is another matter. I have no idea if humans will be any more ethical or moral than they are now. Sadhguru himself hinted in other satsangs about the coming dwapara yuga that humanity, sadly, won't be any more moral or ethical than it currently is. Just because those humans will have more capabilities than we do won't mean they're spiritually and ethically more evolved. It just means they'll have a whole new variety of ways of being a-holes to each other. sigh...

The danger from my perspective is that humanity will take these seemingly 'newly evolved' possibilities as evidence they are ascending to a more permanently perfectible level as a species forgetting that yugas are describing a cycle, not a linear process.

5. There is one other thing of note I've learned from the yogis and tantriks. If you are among those who are scheduled to leave for good it's a huge help to not leave any living DNA on this planet. Specifically don't leave any children after you. It has to do with Agama karma. DNA is a type of Agama karma. To exit this planet permanently at some point all of the Agama has to be spent - all of it - including the DNA you bequeath to any direct descendants. So at some point your bloodline will have to die out for you to be able to leave. The alternative is to take up specific practices that wash off the genetic ties in your own subtle plane body. So now...when I look at say...the Ancient Egyptian pharaohs or Roman emperors - where are their descendants now? Nowhere from what I can tell. So there's a very good chance those kings and their peoples are no longer taking birth on Earth and moved on long ago.

Now look around at the birth rate apocalypse that's taking place all over the planet (even in Africa now!) and you begin to get an idea of just how "cleaned up from excess humans" this planet is going to be again for the new crop of humans coming up from prior incarnations as animals.

So I am not inclined to dismiss JMG's scry. The bit about souls being trapped here - yeah...I've seen other spiritual teachers discuss that as well. It makes me inclined to think there's more than a dollop of truth to it.

comments.title: Manifestation
comments.content:
Hi John,

I wanted to ask you about visualization as it regards to manifestation with our ritual work. From my understanding, in whatever sort of ritual work we are doing, visualizing a successful physical outcome and being able to hold that image in one’s mind is an important part of making it effective.

However, the opposite can often seem to hold true as well. In the domain of human relationships, I’ve taken your advice, and never done any sort of rituals that involve love etc. as I don’t want to challenge another persons free will. However, I have found that on a basic level of fantasy, individuals who I might have a crush on, if I allow myself to fantasize about them, it has the capacity to sabotage the relationship. If I manage to not think about them at all, then somehow their reaction to me is much more positive. I recall in the past, you have mentioned a sort of magical sabotage if one thanks about a particular person while self-pleasuring or orgasm, that makes the individual repelled by you. Is that because it is visualization combined by longing, and the longing is somehow a counterproductive force? That’s a much more explicit example, but even when fantasy extends in other directions, whether it’s the success of a project, financial gains, or a deepening of a friendship, the fantasizing somehow seems to undermine the outcome. I guess the key distinction then is fantasy versus visualization, where fantasy implies a certain longing, that isn’t inherently part of visualization.

So if desire counteracts intention or outcome, how does one “magically will” or manifest an outcome without first desiring that outcome?

replies.title: Re: Manifestation
replies.content:
There's a difference between desiring an outcome and letting yourself fantasize about that outcome. Fantasy -- especially vivid fantasy that moves toward a completion, and even more especially when that completion leaves you with sticky fingers -- absorbs the energy of the desire. Your subconscious mind isn't always clear on the difference between vivid mental imagery and reality, so if you fantasize something in great detail, your subconscious mind will think, "Okay, that's already happened" and stop trying to make it happen.

This is why so many people who get into "manifesting" via visualization and the like fail so reliably. If you want to make something happen, create an abstract image such as a sigil, pour all the desire and emotion you have into the sigil, then release it and forget about it. Do something else -- preferably some practical action that will bring you closer to your goals. Oh, and don't tell anyone about it. In silence is strength!

replies.title: Re: Manifestation
replies.content:
Oh, and don't tell anyone about it. In silence is strength!

Not the OP, but I have seen/heard/read many people say this and I am curious why remaining silent about a goal is helpful. I don't particularly feel the need to discuss my goals or desires with anybody, but I can't understand why it is helpful to not do so, whereas at least talking to people may lead to their direct/indirect help if they are so inclined.

Any insights into this?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Manifestation

Bailey's teachings were taken up during her lifetime, and especially after her death, by a coterie of influential people with close ties to the United Nations, which Bailey herself supported. One of the downsides of the gurucentric organizational structure common to Theosophy and most of its offshoots is that they are vulnerable to political capture -- and indeed there's some evidence that Blavatsky herself was in the pay of the Russian secret police, especially during her time in India.

Personally, I don't find Bailey's teachings especially useful, and oh dear gods, she was verbose! Information received from channeled sources, no matter how exalted the entity claims to be, needs to be judged on the same basis as an anonymous posting online, and by that standard, I have to say DK doesn't stand up that well.

replies.content:
Oh, oh my. That just made me realize in a more intuitive way something I knew in an intellectual way. Maybe not full satori or anything, but oof. I've long been quite comfortable with analyzing narratives, seeing what makes them tick, what should and shouldn't go in certain kinds of narratives to get the desired results, and so forth. replies.title: Re: Toolkit for detecting one's own narraratives?
replies.content:
Pay close attention to any type of Freudian slips, whether in speech, writing, movement, facial expression, or whatever. They are almost always indicative of some area where your soul's/subconscious' take on what's going on lies outside the boundaries of whatever narratives your conscious is using to make sense of the world.

When your hand writes "they" instead of the "she" you intended, when you type "pissed" instead of "thrilled", when your gentle love pat somehow knocks the recipient straight out of their chair, or when your quizzical look reads to others as the most crestfallen face they've ever witnessed, then you can be pretty sure that something not allowed to play any role within your narrative is letting you know that it is actually playing a most potent role. Let that slip tell you its version of events and what it succeeded in picking up on, which your narrative deftly overlooked. Any technique of creative imagination will work well for that, so long as you keep your internal editor sidelined until the slip is done telling its own story.

A similar technique would be keeping a dream journal, which allows your conscious mind to capture and then reflect on whatever stories your soul finds it important to share with your waking self. Dreams can be like little Freudian slips, letting us know that how our larger selves feel about various scenarios might not line up all that well with how our conscious minds would prefer to edit those stories back into its familiar comfort zone. We all tend to freak when we dream about having sex with our parents, even though most of us do end up marrying a close approximation to our mothers or fathers. Our souls find a very useful truth hidden in that disturbing metaphor, while our waking minds run around like chickens with our heads cut off, trying to obliterate the narrative image our wiser souls have so graciously offered us.

— Christophe

comments.title: Weight loss
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'm almost embarrassed to ask this but I suppose you're too knowledgeable to pass up your help out of sheer vanity. I want to lose weight and was wondering whether I could magically assist myself here. I'm not looking for a get-thin-quick spell—common sense suggests that this isn't how these things work but, since common sense is, as you say, rather less common than you'd think, I have confirmed this for myself. (This was a while back when I was messing about with sigils. Once, they day after launching one such sigil, I bought myself lunch at a bakery and was apparently handed my bag upside down, which I realised from the sound of half my lunch hitting the pavement. I can only imagine that that was the universe giving me a slightly exasperated slap and telling me that if I was really serious about this, I'd have to do the work myself.)

A better plan seems to be to journal for the underlying emotional problems, take up regular exercise, and instill better habits with an affirmation. The latter might prove difficult as this problem is rather laden with (negative) emotions for me and you've recommended against tackling such problems with affirmations if one isn't proficient at them—I do have a couple of alternative ideas that could help build up my confidence in the process while possibly also helping in this matter as a second-order effect.

But while these are simple solutions, that's of course not the same as easy, so I'd like to ask about other suggestions I could try in addition to the above. I was particularly wondering about ways to reduce appetite but I'm open to other approaches too.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Weight loss
replies.content:
Hi David
Daily walks / hikes have been the basis of my exercise routine. I was pretty out of shape and had a bit of a belly (not too bad, but not healthy either). Initially i would come home dripping in sweat and have a bit of a sore lower back the next day. That soreness in the lower back disappeared after a couple of weeks and i sweat a lot less these days. But i have to tell you it has made a big difference. I feel a lot better and look a lot better too.

One of the problems i had exercising was the initial first step. And if i had to drive to the gym first it really kept me from exercising. ( i know it doesn't make a whole lot of sense, but it was true) But with walking, all i needed to do was get out the door and i was on my way.

I have also found that going on a nice walk reduces my appetite for couple of hours, gym exercising tends to make me more hungry after the workout.

if this statement wasn't already true for me it might make a good affirmation " I enjoy going on nice long walks. "

(after of few months walking if you want to up your exercise routine, your body will be in better shape to handle the increased stress with as much soreness.)

replies.title: Re: Weight loss
replies.content:
Thanks! I do generally try to walk to and from my train which is a fair bit of distance but I suppose I could add more detours and get out on days I don't need to commute.

As for the affirmation, that's definitely not necessary!

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Weight loss
replies.content:
Start with journaling. Ask yourself questions such as "Why do I want to be fat?" and let yourself write whatever comes to mind in response, without editing or censoring anything. Get a sense of what psychological issues might be feeding (pun not intended) your weight. Then do the same thing with questions such as "Why do I want to be thin?" You have to get the subconscious material squared away before you can do anything constructive with this issue.

Second, get yourself checked out thoroughly by a good alternative health care practitioner. Very often there are unnoticed chronic health issues involved in weight. Diet and exercise only work if the underlying conditions have been addressed. Also, if you're on prescription drugs, make sure none of them have weight gain as a side effect -- a remarkably large number of drugs do this.

Then you can begin ratcheting down your calorie intake and ratcheting up your daily exercise with good results. That's the kind of magic that helps here.

replies.title: Re: Weight loss
replies.content:
Thanks, I'll do that! No prescription drugs, unfortunately—blaming pills would be nicer than blaming myself.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Weight loss
replies.content: Blaming yourself is the most absolute waste of time our species has yet come up with. Get to work on the journaling and talk to a healing practitioner, and find the roots of the problem -- that's useful. Beating yourself up is not.

comments.title: Vesica Piscis Reversed
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

In addition to the weight question, another situation has just turned up—or rather, the situation has been there for a while but until now I've failed to take notice of it. I apologise for the length but to me, all of what I've written seems intrinsically linked to the problem, even if what to exclude might be obvious for someone outside of my head.

You may recall the ghost cat problem I had a few weeks ago. I assume that you field way too many of these questions to remember a detail like this but you recommended I turn to St. Michael for nightly protection, presumably under the very reasonable assumption that my use of the Lord's Prayer meant that I follow some Christian faith.

Technically, I was raised with some Catholic trappings. I even was an altar boy, though mostly because my father implied that this was a mandatory family tradition. However, I never really got it. My mother has since let it slip that she doesn't really believe in God. I'm not sure about my father but he certainly stopped attending mass long before I stopped being an altar boy, which was a long time into my teenage stint as an atheist. Even before that, in my own childish head, God was merely the one I'd have to apologise to whenever I read a book featuring another deity or I'd go to hell, possibly via the express route—I'm still unsure about where I picked up that idea but it certainly contributed to my misguided distaste for religion. But even once I realised that materialism is just as stupid, He's mostly been my go-to term for the Divine and that just because I was raised on Christian terms. I briefly considered going back to mass but then COVID happened and afterwards my own sigil experiments had me so used to incense and candlelight that the one mass I've since attended (my brother's Confirmation), I mostly spent eying the glare of the (semi-functional) electric lights with distaste, thinking that nothing sacred could happen in that room, and was proven quite right when most attendants started photographing the Transubstantiation with their phones.

This probably explains why, truth be told, I had no real idea how to follow your advice. I looked up prayers to St. Michael, scrolled past the AI slop, found the Leonine prayer which sounded good enough, and somewhat formulaically recited it for a few nights before deciding that I might as well not bother and that I was safe enough without it. On the evening after my mirror summoning misadventure, fear inspired a more personal prayer, but I (fortunately) am not usually afraid for my life when going to sleep and so once again stopped bothering.

Then, on the weekend before last, a few things happened while I was on vacation. First, I found a bowl in a shop and was struck by the impression that I should use it for offerings, seeing it filled with a yellow liquid. A day later, I read an essay about someone's relationship with Odin and felt somewhat excited at that thought. I realised that beer and mead are yellow and wondered whether that's a sign. The rest of that day and most of the following, there were ravens everywhere I went (in several cities). I surreptitiously tossed a bit of my steak onto the ground for them to eat and they flew away—the steak dropped rather close to me so I hope they just didn't want to come near me and have returned later but I don't know. That was the last time I've seen a raven.

At home, I wasn't really sure what to do—was this really the invitation that I thought it was or was I just seeing patterns where none are? And if it was, how do I properly respond to it? Or for that matter, do I want to do so at all? The whole thing was still very exciting but I was also quite out of my depth, being raised as a monotheist, and it was too late in the (Mon)day to ask for your help, so I decided to put my hope in A World Full of Gods, placed an order with my bookstore (that still hasn't arrived) and pushed the matter from my mind.

Today, I decided to switch to the faith-neutral version of the SoP. I've been meaning to try this for a while as I struggle to coordinate the invocations and the visualisations and figured that the connection between "by the sky above me" and light descending from the sky is so obvious it might help. This experience has finally pushed me into trying it. I waited for today because I try changing things on Mondays to get full weeks of experiments. It certainly felt better, though a single day of data is obviously not enough for definite conclusions.

In my daily divination, I got

Me: Vesica Piscis reversed (Encounter)
The Situation: Octahedron reversed (Dispersal)
The Outcome: Equilateral Triangle upright (Form)

I originally thought that that this made two separate statements: The situation and outcome show that my attention is jumping between too many different projects and ideas and needs to be channeled into something more concrete and Encounter implies something negative about my interactions with others and a need to take their wishes or perspectives into greater account (based on the fact that this is the Me card and not the Situation card, I interpreted this to speak about a character trait of mine rather than implying I did/will do something specific).

I keep my practice record by recording voice notes immediately after my morning practice or solar plexus exercise (as well as any "extracurricular" activities) and then transcribing them later. This gives me the impression of saving time in the morning. Sometimes, I let several days of notes collect before writing them down (every now and then, I notice that I've had the same problem for a couple of days and actually found a solution during the first but didn't remember it the following days, presumably because I didn't write it down yet, so I know that this is less than ideal). Today, after transcribing my morning exercise, I also wanted to transcribe the last note before that but, apparently, managed to click on the note on the bottom of my screen rather than the one on the top. I heard myself describe with a lot of conviction that, before going to sleep after the mirror misadventure, I asked St. Michael to protect me and that he evidently did because I was alright. Given that I'd just transcribed some musings on religion and that I prefer the SoP without references to Christianity, this had the distinct feeling of St. Michael tapping me on the shoulder and asking "hold on, aren't you forgetting about someone?"

This certainly put the Encounter card into a new perspective—I think I was right in general but made a mistake in assuming it referred to my relationships with humans in my life. Since this seems to be the situation I'm dealing with today, it's quite possible Dispersal and Form refer to my religious practice than my life in general.

I was even less sure how to react to that, so I started typing up this question. Anticipating your most likely answer, I stopped and consulted the SGO. I dealt cards for all of the beings immediately involved (me, St. Michael, Odin), as well as the Christian God (because if I'm wondering about my relation to Christianity, His opinion on the matter is probably relevant too), and all other Gods not immediately involved, because they also seemed tangentially relevant, even if not quite as important.

Me: Hexagram reversed (Conflict)
St. Michael: Golden Proportion reversed (Justice)
Odin: Octahedron upright (Mediation)
The Christian God: Spiral upright (Unfolding)
Other gods: Dodecagram upright (Completeness)

I laughed a little when I dealt the first card—obviously there's conflict, that's the whole point! Unfortunately, I'm less clear on the rest. My initial guess at St Michael's Justice was that he's upset at me, though the guidebook says that it rather means that "whatever it represents has failed," which I don't understand. Has St. Michael failed? In what? I've been rather happy with his protection, if my own note is to be believed. Is it rather my relationship with him that has soured? I have even less of an idea how to read God unfolding.

As for the other gods, my interpretation would ignore the guidebook's interpretation completely and instead go with the very banal "completeness in the sense of looking at everything." At first, they seemed important, but now I'm struggling to come up with a good reason for including them beyond "I'm asking about gods, so let's ask about all of the gods." The answer seems to have been a cosmic shrug: "yup, that's all of 'em."

Which still leaves the most interesting card, Mediation for Odin. Several interpretations come to mind: "your task is to bring [the factors involved] into a relationship that will benefit all concerned," says the book, which might point to me needing to find a way to please both Odin and St. Michael. Then, he reached out to me, which fits the "communication" meaning of mediation. He's possibly an intermediary, though that strikes me as unlikely. Then, if he's the leader of the possessed, that's both a mediatory role between the possessors and the possessed as well as the possessed and others. His association with wisdom would make him a good mediator. One might also make an argument that (good) poetry is mediation between the sacred and the worldly, as well as (no matter its quality) simply a form of communication.

All in all though, I have no idea how to interpret these cards, nor what to do in general.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Vesica Piscis Reversed
replies.content:
As usual when this subject comes up, I have to pitch the work of Galina Krasskova--not only because she wrote two very good beginners' books (Modern Guide to Heathenry and Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner) but because she's a priest of Odin and has written about the Old Man extensively, in books and on her blog "Gangleri's Grove." She holds "Wodinic Wednesdays" for questions, similar to these Magic Mondays. Certainly, read the Eddas, but you might want to start with a retelling such as Kevin Crossley-Holland's Penguin Book of Norse Myths, to get the gist of the stories.

I'd also encourage you to talk to Odin--ask Him for guidance, express your doubts, name your questions, or whatever moves you (and you don't have to call it prayer if that makes you uncomfortable!). You are starting a relationship, and humans build relationships with words as well as gifts (offerings). Just don't forget to also listen for a response, which could be a word placed directly into your mind, or a feeling washing through you...or a book falling on your head, all of which have happened to me and to others on this blog!

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Vesica Piscis Reversed
replies.content: The gods get blamed for a lot of things. Sometimes it's symbolic; I don't think, for example, that Diana actually turned Actaeon into a stag -- his own unclean passions debased him to the animal level, whereupon the consequences of his actions devoured him. Sometimes, as in the Hebrew prophets, you can see the prophet's overinflated ego raging at the people who won't shut up and do what they're told.

replies.title: Re: Vesica Piscis Reversed

replies.title: Remote Viewing Memories and Question on Its Side Effects
replies.content:
If I may, a few thoughts that might be relevant to you here, but I'll hold off on any definite advice, as this is a very personal situation with numerous factors:

1) The late medieval/Renaissance synthesis of Christianity and classical materials acknowledged the existence of at least some of the spiritual beings worshipped as Gods by the ancients (like the planets), but seen in this synthesis as emanations of the higher divinity. This post has an excellent review of C.S. Lewis's book on this topic, The Discarded Image (and, for bonus points, is discussing this book in the exact context of "how do people of the West reconcile our Christian and polytheist roots?" He comes down firmly on the Christian side, but is open to some pagan spice, basically): https://librarianofcelaeno.substack.com/p/an-overlooked-masterpiece-by-c-s

2) For St. Michael specifically, many folks (including our host), have had perfectly good relationships with angels outside of a Christian context. But, perhaps your divination suggests this may not be what's best for you. Whatever happens, from what everyone says, St. Michael is always happy to kick evil's you-know-what, so I'd echo our host's reassurance that even if an ongoing relationship with him is not right for you, I very much doubt there are any hard feelings over him helping and you exploring other options that might work better in the long run.

3) I chuckled a bit reading about your experiences with Odin, as that very much sounds like the Old Man. He has a reputation among Heathens as a "recruiter" - He reaches out to folks to see if they want to give worship of Him and/or the Germanic Gods a try, and sometimes comes on pretty strongly to get your attention. So, to me, it does in fact sound like He was extending an invitation, but as our host has said to lots of other folks, you're under no obligation to take it. Full disclosure, Odin is the God with Whom I feel the closest affinity, and He is very important in my practice, so I might be biased. Also, when I saw "Mediation" my response was "of course." When the Romans equated Odin with a Roman God, it was Mercury. Mediation is one of the key things that links all of the seemingly disjointed things He's all about - mediating between life and death (whether in battle or getting to the afterlife), law and savagery, desires and outcomes, the seen and unseen, the past and the future, the known and the unknown.

4) Should you decide to explore a relationship with Odin, a few steps you can take. Assuming you got the bowl, set up a small altar - a folding table with a white cloth is what I use. Light a candle and place it on the altar (I tend to use a votive in a glass holder or an oil lamp with a wick to avoid wax spillage). Say a short prayer to "open" a holy space - no need for something pre-done, you can just say something like "I open this stead that I may speak with the Holy Ones." If there is another God or spirit that you feel some kinship to related to holy spaces, fire, or boundaries, you might call upon Him or Her in doing this (I ask "Frigg, the Hearthmother" to open mine and pass the fire in a circle around it three times, clockwise). Then, take the bowl, you can have filled it ahead of time with beer, but not so much that it splashes, say something like "I give this beer to Odin as a gift, in the hopes that He share His wisdom with me," and place it on the altar. I make the sign of the hammer (basically an upside down T - start at the top, straight down, to the left, then all the way to the right, like making the sign of the cross, except the crossbar is at the bottom of the vertical line) over drink offerings before offering them, but that's not necessary. Then, with the offered beer on the altar (you could also add incense if you like, since you mentioned it), stand, sit, or kneel before the altar (again, whatever gets you into a properly reverent, but not obsequious or groveling frame of mind), and in plain language, explain what you're thinking, what you'd like help understanding , and so forth. Then, wait, try to open your mind and heart, and "listen" for whatever comes to you - maybe thoughts, maybe feelings, maybe vague impressions. If you get "images" and/or "voices" that you associate with Odin (old man, one eye, charming, but maybe a bit scary, that kind of thing), try to let those come without too much "steering" on your end, and pay attention to what you get. Whenever you feel things are done, say "Thank you" and do something to mark the close of the prayer - I use a word known to have been magically and/or religiously significant to the ancient Germans ("Alu") in the place of "Amen," but I've even seen Heathens recommend just saying "Amen." But not necessary - again, the idea is to support your sense of the sacred, as separated from your daily life. You then might close the altar (something like "I now close this stead for holy work," perhaps while moving the candle around it in a counter-clockwise circle 3 times), blow out the candle (or let it burn until the incense burns out, if you'll be around to catch any surprise fires), step away, and go about your business. Oh, and for the drink offering - I usually try to leave it on the altar for a bit (at least an hour or so if I can), then take it and respectfully dispose of it by either pouring out in the ground outside, or down the drain if needed. I like to offer what's left of it to the House and/or Land Spirits, but that's a preference thing.

Technically, even all of that is not necessary - I only made the offering/altar recommendations because you mentioned the bowl specifically, and because I find altar prayer helpful in my own practice. But as JMG has said lots of times, to pray, all you need to do is close your eyes, address the God or Goddess, and speak (even if only in your mind's voice) as if to a respected elder, then "listen" for responses. I only went into so much detail in case you're like "okay, but what do I actually do?" If you explore a relationship with Odin, take or leave as much of that as you like.

At any rate, good luck sorting all this out, and if you do have any questions about the Germanic Gods or their worship, feel free to reach out by direct message or by leaving a comment on one of my dreamwidth entries.

Cheers,
Jeff

Hello, JMG.

Thanks as always for hosting this forum and freely sharing your wisdom.

Questions:

1) I've taken to heart what you've written about treating others with compassion and fairness no matter how they treat you. In the context of creating as little bad karma as possible, where does self defense fit in? As crises of various sorts loom large on the horizon, I would appreciate your perspective on this topic.

2) I know an elderly woman who is heavily involved in diksha, the Ascended Masters, and the I Am practices. She says that she did the Violet Flame for 35 years. Also, she mentioned in conversation that her mind is often blank or "empty" and that unknown spirits often come in. I'm trying to develop an understanding of what all of this amounts to. Can you share any insights or point me in the direction of free and trustworthy resources I could use to educate myself? I was considering renting her living space within my home, but the outcome card in my Tarot reading was The Fool. I decided not to offer.

Many thanks,
sun_lion

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Self-defense can be done in a compassionate way. If somebody assaults you, you can use precisely the level of force needed to neutralize the attack -- and if what's required is lethal force, yes, that's an option; just make it as clean as you can. I've always admired the samurai practitioners of Zen who pointed out that it's perfectly possible to feel unconditional love for someone when you cut them down with a sword.

2) That's a helluva rabbit hole to go down. The Ascended Masters teachings started as an American offshoot of Theosophy, launched by Guy Ballard in the 1930s, and since then has grown into one of the liveliest fields of American alternative spirituality, distinguished from most of the others by its tendency toward political and cultural conservatism. You can get an overview here:

https://www.saintgermainfoundation.org/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you, JMG!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may, on 1), I've discovered an opportunity to practice this kind of "compassion for an adversary/obstacle" that is far more common for most of us than self-defense: driving (that may not be true for our host, since he never drives, so if he's ever been in a self-defense situation, maybe the reverse!).

Basically, whenever anyone does something stupid, inconsiderate, dangerous, or whatever, my usual reaction is anger (very likely linked to some fear for what those actions might do to me and my driving situation). But, ever since I've taken up the Modern Order of Essenes work, I've tried to respond to as many situations with "blessings" as possible. That led me to start "correcting" my angry response with "But may you be blessed, nonetheless (even if I can't be the one to do it)." In putting this into practice, and thinking about, and wondering whether I'm "really" doing it, or messing it up, and all of that, I've come to realization that it's possible to want the best for the rude/dangerous/merely unexpected driver, even if, on reflection, you still think "that was bad and I wish you didn't do it." Just because someone does something bad doesn't mean you have to want to punish them for it. One nice thing about driving, is that the "proper" response that protects you and/or others is pretty much never "hurt the person doing the bad/dangerous thing," which makes it easier to distinguish between "I wish you hadn't done that" and "I want to make you pay for doing that" (at least intellectually - emotionally it's still often very tangled! At least for me, but I can have a bit of a temper.) With self-defense, it's harder (but maybe, if achieved, even more valuable), to be able to take an action of physical harm, but not because it hurts/punishes, but because it is the solution to the bigger problem (e.g. killing the bad guy who was going to kill innocent people).

Anyway, I have found this a helpful bunny-slope approach to some of these issues, and I share in case it helps anyone else. For me, the seeming paradox of "Even on reflection, I don't want you to be rewarded for this specific behavior, but I do want things to work out to their greatest good for you, in the long run" has helped me to explore some of the angles of all this.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
How long souls spend out of incarnation is very variable. 5-10 years is not uncommon, and neither is 20 -- anything from a few months to a century is well within normal bounds.

As for your daughter, how old is she? Many children remember scraps of their past lives. You might want to write down everything she recalls, as those memories fade out with puberty -- she may want to know about them later.

My parents told me that when I was very young, I didn't want to get into the bath, and made comments consistent with drowning on the Titanic. What's more is that memories have come back to me -- I remember all too well what it's like to not be able to hold my breath anymore, and pull freezing cold water into my lungs as my consciousness faded into black. I'd recommend not drowning if you can help it, it's unpleasant. Not that I can feel it directly, but I'm intellectually aware of it, kind of like seeing it through a pane of glass, almost.
I remember lights on in the ship, and I remember how cold and dark it was. It was definitely the open ocean, nowhere in sight of land. More recently, another memory surfaced, from earlier -- I was on board the ship, and they evacuated the women and kids first, and we had to wait. Part of me resented having to risk my life while they filled the lifeboats, but another part of me knew it was the honorable thing to do. Of course, I ended up as one the unlucky ones, but it happens. Maybe it burned away bad karma, or submerged it rather.

Anyway, the better part of a century passed between that death and when I was born as my current self. I wonder if I was out of incarnation for most of the 20th century? The fact that I remembered that particular death in early childhood suggests to me that that was the most recent incarnation before this one, but is that correct?

comments.title: Qabalistic soul question
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer, and thanks in advance!

I have a question about the Qabalistic divisions of the soul. (I'm coming at this mostly from a Golden Dawn perspective, which may limit my understanding a bit!)

In SL Mather’s early work (pre-GD, specifically "Kabbalah Denudata"), the Nephesch is linked to Yesod. In later, GD documents, Yesod is linked to the Ruach (as are the rest of the sephiroth above Yesod, excepting the Supernal Triad), and the Nephesch is confined to Malkuth. However, in some recent work I believe Yesod is linked to both the Ruach and the Nephesch, and for Malkuth there is the Guph.

In your “Paths of Wisdom," the Guph seems to be the physical body, and not part of the Soul. In contrast, other modern GD writers (e.g., the Ciceros) note that the Guph is separate from the body, "a low level of the subconscious which communicates with the brain about the current condition of the human body." Still others (e.g., Zalewski's “Kabbalah of the Golden Dawn”) claim that "although the Nephesch is the lowest form of the Soul, in one sense there is another form even lower, Guph, the physical body." (I'm sorry about all the name dropping, but I wanted to be clear about my sources.)

So this is all a bit confusing, and I wonder if you could weigh in on a) which division(s) of the Soul are linked to Yesod and Malkuth, and b) the nature of the Guph relative to the Nephesch, and possibly wrt the Etheric Body?

replies.title: Re: Qabalistic soul question
replies.content:
Ask three Cabalists, get at least five answers! As I see it, the physical body is not separate from the soul -- it is the manifestation of the soul on the plane of dense matter. As William Blake wrote, "Man has no Body distinct from his Soul; for that called Body is a portion of Soul discern'd by the five Senses, the chief inlets of Soul in this age."

Thus the material body or guph is Malkuth; the Nephesh is Yesod; the Ruach in the unawakened person is Hod and Netzach, which is why we're so constantly pulled back and forth between reason and emotion; and the Ruach in the awakened person is the five sephiroth from Hod up to Chesed, indicating the considerably greater powers and perceptions that come with the awakening of the Lower Genius or Higher Self.

But if you ask another Cabalist, you'll get a different answer.

comments.title: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Science of Being anon reporting. I’ve been working through the Eugene Fersen’s 1927 correspondence course for a month now, so far it focused on acquiring proficiency with the basic exercises of the system, next month it is going to start introducing specific techniques to improve one’s health before moving on to mental and spiritual exercises. Overall, during the first month my energy level increased, but my emotional state somewhat destabilized, with a noticeable increase in irritability, but nothing I can’t keep under control. Of the exercises covered so far, one in particular, the Star Exercise, feels like it’s supposed to act similarly to a banishing ritual, at least on etheric level, as it produces the feeling of cleanness and thrilling with energy I usually get after performing the Sphere of Protection (but not as intense as SoP) and certain breathwork exercises.

I also found what appears to be an active organization dedicated to teaching Fersen’s system (scienceofbeing.com), but it appears somewhat untrustworthy and cult-like to me. Fersen’s biography is particularly ridiculous, with claims of aristocratic origin that fail to stand up to any scrutiny, and a list of people his teachings allegedly influenced, that would require time-travel on his part (I suppose the connection work the other way around in actuality). It also has a list of exercises covered in the correspondence course (I refrained from reading through it in its entirety for the sake of replicating the original experience of getting a new packet of instructions every week), and instructions for some of those read like a word salad. I suppose I should stick around with the course, if only for the sake of training of the will, and see if they make sense when I get to them.

As for my experiments with the SoP, I cut them short, as the modified version kept producing unpleasant sensation and felt wrong on the intuitive level. One of the reason I wanted to try this experiment was a passage in Gareth Knight’s book on ceremonial magic that implied that the four elements used by Western magicians are, in a way, arbitrary, and as long as there is a magical circle and the magician establishes themselves as it’s center, they can tap into any meaningful polarity to perform work. I guess I confirmed this for myself in a way, even if what I did didn’t work in the way I wanted it to.

I think I also traced the color scale Fersen used to its origin in the Johann Wolfgang von Goethe’s Theory of Colors. Quite a fascinating book. For what its worth, the other occult author that seemingly made use of Goethe’s color wheel is Harry J. Gardener. There are few other aspects of Fersen’s teachings that superficially remind me of what I’ve read of Gardener’s works, but no enough to make a connection.

Thank you for hosting Magic Monday and best wishes to you and fellow posters!

replies.title: Re: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
replies.content: Thank you for this! I've learned a lot from Gardener, oddly enough, so this makes me even more interested in Fersen's material.

In the law of acceptance, there are two procedures given to aid in acceptance. comments.title: Brown as Dark Orange
comments.content:
I have no question this week, but I'd like to share something I initially found mildly amusing, but on reflection realized is actually very significant: I was watching a YouTube video* on colour perception. The video was entirely about how weird brown is, because it's actually "objectively" dark orange (or "Orange with context"). I spent a while trying to see the world as if that was subjectively the case as well, and found it surprisingly challenging.

I was aware, intellectually, of how much pre-processing happens below the level of conscious awareness, but some of the demonstrations from that video hammered home the point in a very visceral way: I think the fact that brown is really just "orange with context", paired with evidence that it is the case, is now one of the best ways I'm aware of for demonstrating how not objective our sense of the world really is.

*link for those interested: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wh4aWZRtTwU

replies.title: Re: Brown as Dark Orange
replies.content:
This reminds me of two of the most fascinating color facts:

  1. Magenta is not a color of light. It’s an artifact of the eyes receiving a mix of colors that should combine to yellow but since our yellow cones don’t fire our brains know it isn’t yellow so it makes up a color.

  2. There are colors we can define mathematically but can’t see, specifically colors with full saturation but either no luminance (so they appear black) or full luminance (so they appear white). But you can trick your brain into seeing them in an afterimage.

replies.title: Re: Brown as Dark Orange
replies.content: 1) That seems very odd to me, because I see magenta as halfway between violet and red -- thus red with a little blue in it, as distinct from violet (equal amounts of red and blue) or indigo (blue with a little red in it).

replies.title: Re: Brown as Dark Orange
replies.content:
Those sound like garbled versions of the following color facts:

1. Most of the colors "between" red and blue don't correspond to a pure frequency of light. The brain constructs color perceptions from the responses of three kinds of cones. The responses from each kind of cone vary smoothly with the frequency of the light, so when you have two light sources in pure frequencies that are close to each other, you can replace them almost perfectly with a single light source with a frequency that's in the middle -- at most it'll just look slightly more pure-colored. The farther apart the starting frequencies are, though, the more washed-out their mixed color looks, until if you do this with two frequencies that have opposite positions on the color wheel, you get something like white. (I say "something like white", because thinking of "white" as a well-defined reference color that illumination can have is only a bit better than thinking of "mutt" as a well-defined breed that a dog can have.) And if you go even further, and try to do this with pure-frequency red light and blue light, which are almost as far apart as you can get, you come out somewhere on the opposite side of white -- the in-between frequency might be green or yellow, and purple or magenta are the opposite of that. By the laws governing the three-dimensional space of variations of responses from the three kinds of cones and the smoothness of that kind of neural processing, there still has to be some visual perception that corresponds to a mix of pure-red light with pure-blue light, so why not call it purple or magenta?

However, there is something a little further along than blue. If something looks like it might be a pure frequency (or at least single-temperature) source of light, but also looks sort of near purple in an uncanny indigo-except-also-somehow-vaguely-hot-pinkish ("actinic") way, don't look directly at it! It might be intense ultraviolet, that only looks like an unthreatening intensity of light because your retina can barely register it. Depending on the intensity and wavelengths, it might give your cornea ( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photokeratitis ) or retina ( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photic_retinopathy ) something like sunburn. I don't know the exact ranges for danger, though, other than that arc-welding sparks are dangerous at typical worker ranges, staring directly at the sun is dangerous without surprising amounts of dimming filters, you can temporarily sunburn your cornea and get snowblindness by having a visual field full of sunlit snow for a few hours, and it's also a mistake to stare at blacklights. (Regular greenhouse lights that are purple are safe, if you know they aren't trying to include much ultraviolet; they just leave out green light for efficiency's sake because (most?) plants reject it anyway.)

2. The colors you can trick your brain into seeing using perceptual fatigue and afterimages are called "chimerical colors" ( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impossible_color#Chimerical_colors ). You can get colors that are subjectively darker than black but still faintly saturated; these are called "stygian colors". You can get colors that are subjectively brighter than subtractive-white but still faintly saturated; these are called "self-luminous colors" because they seem to subtract even less light than subtractive-white does, i.e. they seem to glow with their own light. And you can get subjectively hypersaturated colors, called "hyperbolic colors". I don't think this method lets you get fully saturated colors near black or white, though; afterimages are too faint to reach fully-saturated from a non-saturated starting point.

And how about this:

3. The red-green color axis is more dramatic in consciousness than it is in the difference of the responses of the cones it's built from. To construct the red-green axis, the brain amplifies small differences between the responses of two proteins that respond both to red and to green, where one of them (in "L" cones, for Long-wavelength) responds a bit more to redder light, and the other of them (in "M" cones, for Medium-wavelength) responds a bit more to greener light. These proteins are coded on the X chromosome. (That's why chromosomally male humans are more vulnerable to red-green color blindness: they don't have a backup source of a light-detecting protein on a second X chromosome, if the first source is broken, and so the red-green axis is lacking one of its anchors to push against and spread itself out.) However, these proteins exist in subtly different versions; the different versions have even finer distinctions in frequency responses from each other than the differences between M cone proteins the L cone proteins. Since chromosomally female humans have half their cells with one of their X chromosomes active and another half with the other X chromosome active, they can end up with different cone cells manifesting two different variations for one or both of the proteins, and so become able to actually perceive four dimensions of color variation (though this is a subtle effect and hard to conclusively demonstrate outside a lab), or even five (though I'm not sure anyone's ever done the experiments to distinguish this from four). Conversely, even people with "normal" three-color vision can still perceive what is optically speaking the same world in different colors, because different people have different versions of these subtle mutations.

4. There's supposed to be a way to train perception of etheric aura by wearing goggles that block out everything but a certain band of the near ultraviolet, to screen out distractions. Presumably, since the aura light isn't detected by machines, the mechanism would be that the etheric aura emits some kind of abstractly photon-like excitations that are affected by artificial dyes as though they were photons in the near-ultraviolet range, and that somehow piggyback on the normal causal pathways for specifically-biological photon processing to produce light-perception-like effects. However, on the basis of available inductive evidence, this seems likely to be another of those kinds of paranormal perceptual phenomena that are, at best, entirely susceptible to unannounced "void where prohibited" sorts of cosmological rugpulls, and accordingly are carefully kept close to a plausible-deniability cover story of perceptions that the subject could have taught themselves to confabulate, in order to facilitate such rugpulls should they later prove necessary. It was apparently not so hard for a team publishing in the BMJ in 1912 to convince themselves, or at least claim to have convinced themselves, that they'd tried to replicate Kilmer's original reported method in good faith and gotten null results. (It wouldn't have helped that Kilmer confidently reported that the aura was a part of ordinary physics without any subjectivity-laden funny business, suggesting that the replication team wouldn't have had any reason to try to control for any confounding explanations about the effect getting rugpulled from having been available to them because of their not having cultivated the right subjective belief-like states, any more than one should expect that any other effect involving ordinary, almost-subjectivity-proof physical particles could get rugpulled like that.)

5. This past year in Berkeley, there have been experiments to use subject-retina-specific structured light fields to artificially stimulate just M cones, and produce a new subjective color dubbed "olo" (from the shapes of the numerals for 0 in long-wavelength cones, 1 in medium-wavelength cones, and 0 in short-wavelength cones; presumably hyper-S color would be called "ool" and hyper-L color would be called "loo"). This color is reported subjectively as being an intense blue-green, more saturated than any stable naturally-perceivable color. This is just what you'd respect, since the brain constructs a red-green color axis by trying to amplify it from out of some subtle response differences, but the actual response differences had been scientifically engineered to be as unsubtle as possible. The description of olo reminds me of some experiences I've had with aura-migraine-like momentary visual blobs of multiple extreme simultaneous conflicting colors; one of the conflicting colors was about a quarter of the way from fluorescent-cyan to what it sounds like olo would be.

(It's weird how the news about olo parallels a bit of the other comment this week about Sadhguru's claims about the next thousand years.)

replies.title: Re: Brown as Dark Orange
replies.content: 4) I tend to assume that any pseudoskeptical "refutation" of paranormal phenomena is fake. That doesn't mean that the paranormal phenomena exist -- just as it's possible for the police to frame a person who's actually guilty -- but pseudoskeptics from the Unamazing Randi on down have been caught engaging in hanky-panky and bad logic so many times that at this point, their testimony ranks down there with Spiritualist mediums. As I continue my research into radionics, I hope to acquire some proper Kilner goggles and give it a try myself.

replies.title: Re: Brown as Dark Orange
replies.content: One experiment would be to see where aura perceptions fall on a spectrum from simple perceptions mediated by exotic particles, to phantasm/apparition-like effects mediated by a pervasive infrastructure of space-occupancy-consciousness, by testing which phenomena of ordinary optics it is that aura para-light seems to follow. For example, perhaps aura para-light images are displaced by lenses and reflected by glancing contacts with surfaces where refractive indexes drop, but are not reflected by silvered mirrors (which normally reflect photons by their coupling to electronic oscillations called plasmons)... well, actually that would be ambiguous with respect to its implications about the phantasm/apparition hypothesis, I guess.

replies.content: Done by someone who had no axe to grind, that would be very valuable. I'm not sure I qualify, but I did a bit of searching, found that genuine dicyanin Kilner glasses are available, and ordered a pair. So I'll be able to settle the question to my own satisfaction in due time!

replies.content:

Maybe something with a pure-quartz prism, a dark room, a small light-bulb-style incandescent bulb (are those even still allowed for sale in the US?) or fairy-light-style tiny incandescent bulb (you can probably get those), a dark room, a digital camera on a stable tripod set to long exposure, some fluorescent dye, a reference source for the absorption spectrum of dicyanin, and maybe someone you know to help with the digital image processing if the spectra can't simply be eyeballed.

comments.title: Earth and blood
comments.content:
In lieu of a question for this week, I have a report. I accidentally bumped into one of the major reasons that I had to discontinue practising the SoP, which also went on to explain why I was having some of the reactions to the elements that I was.

I had been starting my days with the SoP for a few months (I couldn't find my journals with the exact dates) and I had noticed a number of interesting effects. One being that I had very different reactions to each of the elements - Air and Water were mostly neutral, Fire was a major struggle, and I LOVED how Earth made me feel. I would barely get the words, "By the green gate..." out of my mouth when a big WHOOSH of shivers and tingles would come over me. I greatly looked forward to this feeling.
However, things began to take a turn not long after I started to experience these larger, more noticeable sensations. Eventually I found myself having to pause and rest in between each element. A growing feeling of 'air hunger' was also a big warning sign for me. Divination and common sense confirmed that I needed to stop.

In my mundane life outside of all workings, I had always struggled with a slew of inconsistent and perplexing symptoms. By all accounts I'm in great health - no deficiencies, diseases or injuries have ever been found - any yet I still have days where I find myself laid out, exhausted, weak, heart racing, dizzy...
But this week I tried something different, and was floored at how immediately effective it was: Salt. As in, eating large amounts of table/sea salt straight from my salt dish. My symptoms quickly resolved, and I felt healthy. More importantly, I felt ALIVE! With this discovery, the murky outline of my problem finally started to come into view:

Hypovolemia. I don't have enough blood/plasma in my body.
(And I believe this is a purely etheric phenomenon. A hypothesis I'm still working on)


I loved working with the Earth element because it's quite literally what I needed. Salt, the de facto symbol of the earth element, increases fluid retention, which in turn increases blood volume.
While Fire, the etheric element (unless I'm mistaken?) was putting pressure on an already haggard system. Heat and the accompanying sweating are not good for a person with critically low blood pressure/volume.
It all makes sense!

I don't believe I'll live to see the day that esoteric tools will be used to diagnose health matters as readily as we currently use physical means, but this experience has shown me that it's very possible. :)

I would never have made these discoveries if I hadn't found this little corner of the internet, so I wanted to thank you, JMG, from the bottom of my struggling little heart, for making so much useful information so readily available.

replies.content: That's fascinating. Be aware that a growing body of research suggests that the demonization of salt by the medical industry and popular culture has caused serious health problems in a lot of people!

comments.title: Baptism and nature spirits

A couple of years ago, you mentioned that those baptised in the Catholic Church in the medieval period, when the egregore was still very strong, would have had trouble perceiving nature spirits and working certain kinds of magic (although this effect seems to have dissipated now).

Luke

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content: The kinds of magic that people traditionally couldn't work any more after baptism are the ones that require seeing and communicating with nature spirits. replies.content: May I ask how we know this? Is it old accounts of magicians being baptized and burning their books, or their spells no longer working, or something else?

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
Yes, exactly. There are plenty of old accounts from the early Middle Ages of baptism and other Christian rites being stronger than magic, and driving spirits away. Consider this bit from Chaucer:

"In th' olde dayes of the Kyng Arthour,
Of which that Britons speken greet honour,
Al was this land fulfild of fayerye.
The elf-queene, with hir joly compaignye,
Daunced ful ofte in many a grene mede.
This was the olde opinion, as I rede;
I speke of manye hundred yeres ago.
But now kan no man se none elves mo,
For now the grete charitee and prayeres
Of lymytours and othere hooly freres,
That serchen every lond and every streem,
As thikke as motes in the sonne-beem,
Blessynge halles, chambres, kichenes, boures,
Citees, burghes, castels, hye toures,
Thropes, bernes, shipnes, dayeryes --
This maketh that ther ben no fayeryes."

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
Fitting this into your vision you have been speaking of concerning the roughly 1,000,000,000 souls from an etheric planet;
I always found it bizarre that we as a species, specifically Western cultures, disconnected ourselves so thoroughly from the nature of Earth. Was baptism potentially a way of making the Earth safe for these recent arrivals? Did the faery world pose a threat to their existence somehow? Can we invite faeries back to dance among humans once again?

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content: As I see it, Western civilization had the task of figuring out everything that our species could do with matter in a purely manipulative, rational way; cutting our intuitive and emotional connection with the world was essential so that we could do that. We paid a bitter price for that, but now future civilizations will be able to take what we learned and do less alienating things with it. My guess is that the fairies never actually went anywhere -- we just stopped being able to see them -- and as that state of self-induced blindness ends, we'll realize that they're still here.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
Interesting! That suggests that it's not just Baptism but Roman Catholic rites of blessing generally.

I wonder if the same is true of other Christian traditions? Orthodox monks seem to have good relationships with nature spirits, if their reportedly-common friendships with wild animals are anything to go by...

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content: Medieval Catholic saints had the same kind of good relationships with animals, interestingly enough.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
In addition to what JMG posted, one point of history that made rounds in Christian circles a few years ago is that when the Spanish began their conquest of the Aztecs, the priests of the Aztec religion used their magic against the invading conquistadors and were genuinely shocked when it didn't work.

The Christian egregore -- entered into through baptism -- for a long time seemed to suppress competing powers wherever it held sway.

And this arguably extends to at least Judaism as well: consider the story in 1 Kings 18:25-39, where Elijah challenges the priests of Baal to sacrifice a bull and call upon their god to start a fire, which he fails to do, then Elijah prays to Yahweh to start a fire, and a raging blaze starts right up. This is generally taught to Christians these days as proving that Baal doesn't really exist, but there's an alternative interpretation: the god of Abraham will not tolerate competition wherever he holds sway.

This seems to have changed sometime in the last century or two, and the Christian powers in particular seem much more tolerant of polytheism these days. Then again, it would be interesting to see if non-Abrahamic powers are still suppressed near Jerusalem -- perhaps this is a matter of jurisdictional boundaries changing with the changing ages.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
It gets better. Baal was the lord of the sky who brought the rains. But Jehovah & Elijah sealed up the heavens so there wouldn't be enough water vapor in the air to produce dew, much less rain. Jehovah took Baal's domain away from him.

However, the Canaanites explained drought by having Baal be dragged into the underworld by his enemy Death. He takes down the rainclouds to blackmail the other gods into rescuing him from the underworld lest the whole world perish in famine.

replies.title: Re: Baptism and nature spirits
replies.content:
Thank you for this! Yeah, over the past few millennia, the god of Abrahama clearly claimed much of the world for his own as his followers spread his various faiths.

No hard feelings from me though: inter-deity conflict is normal in any mythology, and no business of mine which side gets the upper hand for a while.

replies.title: Re: Baptism
replies.content:
It's fascinating to me that I received two questions about baptism in a row! I think that it would be a good thing; in the early Middle Ages, when Christianity's egregor was still strong, baptism shut down connections with nature spirits and certain kinds of Pagan magic, but that hasn't been true for many centuries. My late wife, who was baptized into the Catholic church before the Vatican II "reforms" sucked the life blood out of it, was a gifted clairvoyant who had no trouble at all interacting with nature spirits; for that matter, Dion Fortune was baptized into the Anglican Church -- again, using the old robust rite -- and spent much of her career working with nature spirits as well as with Christian saints and angels.

All in all, then, I don't think there's anything wrong with it, and the protection of angels and saints is worth having.

comments.title: Remote Viewing Memories and Question on Its Side Effects
comments.content:
Good Afternoon,

Long time, no ask, but I came across something I thought folks here might enjoy, even if our host doesn't (it's a video), but I suspect he'll be able to answer the question it raised for me nonetheless. As the moon begins waxing, may you find more of the things you want more of, and not of those you don't.

To Share: From a rather unexpected, non-occult direction, I came across a link to this short (~24 minute) video on remote viewing - how it was developed in the military intelligence community, commercialized by the guy who led the development, and what happened to some of the core folks involved. Very interesting and takes a very neutral stance on how seriously to take it, the claims of its proponents, and so forth. There's also a certain amount of human drama revealed, which is linked to my question below, so if you don't want spoilers, skip the question until you've seen it: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gyop46Nz9G4

To Ask: So, mostly for our host, but always interested if others have thoughts. From what I understand of remote viewing, it's basically just a specific form of traditional clairvoyance, which I understand to be a specialized form of scrying, just focused on using the astral light to view material things, rather than more purely astral things as you might, if say, scrying one of the elemental gates. JMG, in the past, you've said that a good way to judge spiritual figures is what their own lives look like - if it's a dumpster fire, stay away from what he's teaching! While the video didn't make it seem like Major Dames (whom didn't you say you had met?) got quite to "dumpster fire," there was definitely a lot that doesn't look great for his life (leaving his first wife for the one that became his business partner, her leaving him for her new business partner, both of them losing the company and all their money when that new guy backstabbed them, etc). So, while parts of the video had me thinking "maybe I should check out remote viewing," by the end I was like "but not however they were selling it."

All of which is to set up the question: do you know enough to comment on how much this seeming spiritual fallout came from the use of the technique itself? Because if it's just regular clairvoyance, that seems odd, since that's been practiced by lots of occultists with fine track records (along with some of the bad ones of course), or do you think it came from something else? One hypothesis that came to mind is that all of them turned the technique almost exclusively to practical ends (finding military targets, making money, looking for UFOs), with varying levels of ethicality (Dames seems to have been trying to do honorable work in the military, he might have gotten a bit off track building the business, and then his UFO search may have had spiritual/ethical dimensions for him, whereas the guy who ended up with the original company seems to have been a pure, greedy huckster). So, am I on the right track, or do you think something else is up?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Remote Viewing Memories and Question on Its Side Effects
replies.content:
No, I don't know Major Edward Dames. I knew General Albert Stubblebine -- in fact, he was one of the three Gnostic bishops who assisted at my consecration. (He was the original of "General Dean Hopgood" in the movie The Men Who Stare At Goats. He did a better job of psychic spoon-bending than anyone else I've ever seen.)

As for your question, psychism is risky to use without a solid ethical foundation and some sort of connection to the Divine. Bad luck like that suggests that they'd lost their ethical compass and were getting thwacked by the raspberry jam principle.

replies.title: Re: Remote Viewing Memories and Question on Its Side Effects
replies.content:
Ah, my mistake! I think the fact that I had forgotten the names of the folks involved meant that I conflated two different army officers involved in the program.

And yeah, that makes sense. And it's at least partially supported by the idea that Major Dames's fortunes seemed to be going okay when he got out of the Army and started the business with the idea of sharing a useful tool with people who needed it, declined when he started cutting corners to make as much money from doing that as possible, and have seemingly stabilized (at least from what was presented in the video) as he has found a more workable balance between "share what I know for others' good" and "get paid enough to make a living from them." Whereas most of the worst stuff that happened to his now ex-second wife and the man she married after him were after doubling down on "let me use these powers to make my own life better" (interestingly, she used remote viewing to identify her "perfect mate," and it was the guy who ended up stealing the company from her and that apparently until the end of her life lived in fear was sending armed men to stalk her, whether that was true or her own paranoid delusions is unclear).
